pattern: Since only the first D(i) copies of an item ever unlock a fresh free item (worth double per coin spent) and every copy after that is worth the same single unit as buying the globally cheapest item, spend budget on the best-ratio double-value copies first and dump whatever remains into cheap single-value filler.
hints: For item i, buying its k-th copy unlocks a NEW free item only while k is at most D(i), the number of other items whose factor is a multiple of i's factor -- beyond that cap, extra copies of i are worth exactly one item each, same as any other purchase.

A 'double' copy (cost = item's price, worth 2 items) is only worthwhile compared to just buying two cheap filler copies at the globally lowest price when its price does not exceed twice that lowest price -- anything pricier than that should be skipped in favor of filler.

Group the worthwhile double-value copies by price, process the cheapest price group first spending as much budget as it can absorb before moving to the next group, and once every profitable double copy is exhausted, spend all remaining budget on copies of the single cheapest item.

edge_inputs.description: buying two copies of a low-factor item unlocks two different free items
several items compete for budget between double-value and filler purchases
a single item, purely filler purchases
budget too small to buy even one copy of the cheapest item
an expensive item whose double-slot ratio is worse than filler should be skipped entirely in favor of filler
two items share the exact same factor, so each purchase of one unlocks a free copy of the other
an item with factor 1 has an enormous number of double slots since it divides every other factor

stress.description: moderate random items and budget to cross-check against the reference oracle
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 400))
    items = [[rng.randint(1, 50), rng.randint(1, 80)] for _ in range(n)]
    budget = rng.randint(1, 3000)
    return [items, budget]

stress.description: larger item count with a moderate budget, still small enough for the brute oracle to cross-check
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 1800))
    items = [[rng.randint(1, n), rng.randint(1, 60)] for _ in range(n)]
    budget = rng.randint(1, 2500)
    return [items, budget]

complexity.time: O(maxFactor log maxFactor + n log n)
